details.diagnosis: The task is to assess the likely AIE mechanism from the provided SMILES in an initial, non-final screening round. From SMILES alone, the current top1 working hypothesis is ICT and the top2 runner-up is TICT. ESIPT also remains a credible portfolio member because the structure contains [nH]-bearing aza-heteroaromatic motifs that could, in principle, participate in proton-transfer chemistry if favorable geometry exists; neutral aromatic remains a weaker fallback. ICT is only a working hypothesis because the same extended conjugation and heteroaromatic substitution that suggest charge redistribution also coexist with a crowded multi-aryl topology that could support torsion-coupled excited-state behavior, and SMILES alone does not establish whether any intramolecular H-bonded proton-transfer geometry is actually preorganized. Shared prepared structure context is available for downstream execution, but per the initial-stage rule it is treated here only as readiness, not as first-layer mechanism evidence. Macro can provide one deterministic bounded structural screen from the registry, but cannot make a global mechanism decision. Microscopic can perform exactly one low-cost baseline S0/S1 action on the shared prepared structure, which can provide indirect excited-state evidence but not conformer sensitivity, torsion sensitivity, or a full mechanistic closure. Both macro and microscopic evidence should be gathered in round 1 because macro can efficiently characterize structural propensity relevant to TICT/rotor-driven alternatives, while microscopic can check whether the lowest excited-state behavior looks compatible with substantial charge-transfer character. The case remains in portfolio screening rather than pairwise contraction because at least one still-credible third hypothesis, ESIPT, has not yet been explicitly screened or ruled out.
